articles.headline: Mine safety stories
articles.summary: Journalists utilized computer-assisted reporting techniques to investigate mine safety in the wake of the Sago Mine disaster, leveraging data from the U.S. Mine Safety and Health Administration.

articles.headline: Data lifts lid on DeLay money
articles.summary: Analysis of financial contributions and ethics violations reveals insights into the influence and power held by Rep. Tom DeLay in Congress.

articles.headline: Advanced GIS analysis targets lost wetlands
articles.summary: A GIS-based investigation discovers large-scale loss of wetlands in Florida, underscoring the failure of federal commitments to protect these critical areas.

articles.headline: Campus crime
articles.author_title: NICAR and Missouri School of Journalism
articles.summary: An analysis of campus crime statistics highlights the University of Massachusetts as having the highest rate of violent crime, prompting discussions on safety measures.

articles.headline: Simplifying database full-text searches
articles.summary: Introduction of OpenInfo, a tool for intranet-based full-text search across newsroom databases, offering a cost-effective and efficient solution for data access.

articles.headline: Yes, we still say "computer-assisted reporting"
articles.author_title: NICAR and Missouri School of Journalism
articles.summary: Computer-assisted reporting remains a vital journalistic skill that goes beyond simple web searching, involving data analysis through databases and spreadsheets to uncover stories that wouldn't be apparent from surface-level web browsing.

articles.headline: Truck inspections
articles.summary: The Database Library has updated the U.S. Department of Transportation truck inspection dataset through 2004, allowing journalists to investigate truck safety violations by company, state, and cargo type. The resource enables reporting on enforcement gaps, unsafe trucking companies, and industry lobbying efforts.

articles.headline: Finding surprises in drug arrest files
articles.summary: A journalist describes how computer-assisted reporting training helped uncover a drug trend in a Kentucky town by analyzing police data, emphasizing that successful data journalism requires both technical skills and strong source relationships.

articles.headline: Covering Congress, states getting trickier
articles.summary: Computer-assisted reporting on elections has become more complex due to campaign finance law changes, requiring journalists to track data from multiple sources including 527 committees and state PACs beyond just the FEC database. Reporters covering 2006 elections must learn to gather and integrate federal, state, and IRS data to avoid missing important stories about campaign funding.

articles.headline: Simplifying database full-text searches
articles.summary: OpenInfo is a free, open-source intranet solution that enables newsrooms to conduct simultaneous full-text searches across multiple databases without expensive tools or extensive coding, featuring customizable search forms and cross-referencing capabilities built on Apache, MySQL, and PHP.

articles.headline: Mapping Data for News Stories
articles.summary: A journalist used data analysis and geocoding to map the geographic coverage of emergency warning sirens in Peoria County, finding that 97% of residents are within siren range, and also mapped schools and nursing homes to assess their siren coverage.

articles.headline: Network analysis of city's power lines
articles.author_title: editor of the projects team at the Richmond Times-Dispatch; former deputy director of the Virginia Public Access Project
articles.summary: Journalists used UCINET network analysis software to map Richmond's power structure through board interlocks, revealing a tightly connected "good-old-boy" network of wealthy elites, while grappling with how to incorporate newly elected Mayor L. Douglas Wilder into their visualization.
